items.myth: More neurons always means a better model.
items.reality: Wider layers increase capacity but also parameters and overfitting risk. Validation performance often peaks at a moderate width; extra neurons can hurt generalization without enough data or regularization.
items.example: On a small tabular dataset with 500 rows, a hidden width of 4096 may memorize training labels while width 32 generalizes better on held-out data.

items.myth: The input layer has learnable weights like hidden layers.
items.reality: In the standard MLP picture, the input layer simply presents feature values; the first learnable weight matrix sits between the input vector and the first hidden layer (or output layer if there are no hidden tiers).
items.example: For 784 pixel inputs, the first nn.Linear(784, 256) owns 784*256 weights — the input tier itself is not a Linear module in most frameworks.

items.myth: Depth and width measure the same thing — both just mean "bigger network."
items.reality: Width is parallel capacity at one stage (how many features computed simultaneously). Depth is sequential transformation (how many stacked stages). A wide shallow net and a narrow deep net can have similar parameter counts but different inductive biases.
items.example: 784→512→10 (depth 1, wide) vs 784→64→64→64→10 (depth 3, narrow) — different shapes, different training dynamics, not interchangeable by neuron count alone.
